title: The Promise of Independence
lede: Marking 250 years of American independence through one Long Island town: its land, its people, and a promise still being kept. Seven short chapters, in English and Spanish.
intro: This exhibit follows the promise of independence through the Town of Islip, one panel at a time, from the land before the town had a name to the community it is today.
Read it straight through, or open any single panel on its own. Each has its own web address, so a panel can later live on a printed sign in a library, opened with a phone.
panelsHint: Read in order, or jump to any chapter.
draftNotice: This text is an early draft, under review. Wording may change, and some panels note where a claim rests on a secondary or local source.
sourcingNotice: This panel is still being written. Its text is a placeholder and is waiting on confirmed sources, so nothing here should be quoted or treated as final yet.
translationPending: This panel's English text is drafted; the Spanish translation is being prepared. You can read the drafted English version in the meantime.

the-promise.inBrief: This exhibit asks a big question: what did the promise of freedom in 1776 really mean, and who was included in it? We follow that question through one town, Islip, on Long Island.
the-promise.title: The Promise
the-promise.summary: What independence promised, and the question this exhibit asks.
sources: New York State 250th Commemoration Field Guide.
body: The 250th anniversary of American independence is not only a celebration of 1776. In New York, it is also an invitation to look honestly at the Revolution: what it promised, whom it included, and whom it left outside its ideals of liberty and freedom. The New York State 250th Commemoration Field Guide frames the anniversary around both New York's role in the Revolution and the continuing struggle of marginalized groups to reach those ideals.

This exhibit begins with that question and brings it to one Long Island town. In Islip, the promise of independence can be followed through land, law, occupation, family stories, town records, and the lives of people who kept making a home here after the war. The question is not only what independence meant in 1776, but what it has meant to each generation since.

The story is told as two pillars joined by one bridge. The first pillar is Revolutionary Islip: the land before the town, the creation of local government, the choice to sign the Articles of Association, the town's own militia company, six years of British occupation, Sagtikos Manor, and Washington's 1790 visit. The second pillar is the unfinished promise: the people who were excluded, the enslaved and indentured workers, and the communities that later widened who belongs in Islip.

before-the-town.inBrief: Islip did not begin on a single day. It grew from Native land, to a colonial land purchase in 1683, to a local government in 1710. Its story started long before it was called a town.
before-the-town.title: Before the Town
before-the-town.summary: Why Islip has no single founding date.
sources: George J. Munkenbeck, “The Origins of the Town of Islip.”
Carl A. Starace, ed., Book One of the Minutes of Town Meetings and Register of Animal Ear Marks of the Town of Islip, 1720–1861 (Town of Islip, 1982); digitized by the Office of the Town Historian.
body: The Town of Islip did not begin on one simple founding date. The 1683 purchase that became “Islip Grange” was a land purchase, not the founding of the town government. The original deed came from Winnaquaheagh, Sachem of the lands in today's Islip Town, to William Nicoll, and it described the land through rivers, shoreline, and boundary lines, a reminder that colonial claims were mapped onto land that already held Indigenous history and meaning.

Local government came later. In 1710 a colonial act allowed the Precinct of Islip to elect its own assessors, a collector, a constable, and a supervisor, and to keep its own public accounts. That act, not the 1683 purchase, is the real beginning of Islip's government.

By 1720 the town records show power tied closely to land. The first election involved 28 freeholders, that is, qualified voters who were male property holders, and four of the five officials elected shared surnames with patentee families. So Islip's origin is layered: Indigenous land, colonial purchase, royal patent, precinct government, and family power. The promise of independence would later arrive in a place where questions of land, authority, and belonging already ran deep.

The town's first minute book shows what that early government actually did. Every April, at the annual town meeting, neighbors elected a supervisor, a clerk, assessors, a collector, a constable, overseers of the poor, overseers of the highways, and fence viewers. They made rules about hogs and boars running loose, kept the town's accounts to the penny, and paid for the care of poor neighbors, like Hannah Hulse, boarded at a set weekly rate in 1739. Long before the Revolution, running the town was already a shared, yearly habit.

revolution-comes-to-islip.inBrief: When the American Revolution reached Long Island, it came right into Islip's homes and farms. Isaac Thompson of Sagtikos Manor had to protect his family while the British occupied the area for six years.
revolution-comes-to-islip.title: Revolution Comes to Islip
revolution-comes-to-islip.summary: How the Revolution entered Islip's homes and farms.
sources: Town of Islip Historian's Office, “A 250th Story.”
George J. Munkenbeck, “Isaac Thompson — A Man on a Tightrope.”
Michele Antonio, “George Washington Slept at Sagtikos Manor,” Patch, June 26, 2010 (updated June 27, 2010). Secondary local-history source.
“The Islip Precinct Articles of Association,” May 10, 1775.
Robert Finnegan, “Islip Militia: 250th Anniversary, 1776–2026,” The Quahog, Winter 2026.
Carl A. Starace, ed., Book One of the Minutes of Town Meetings and Register of Animal Ear Marks of the Town of Islip, 1720–1861 (Town of Islip, 1982); digitized by the Office of the Town Historian.
body: When the American Revolution reached Long Island, Islip could not stand apart from it, and the choice came early. On May 10, 1775, weeks after the fighting at Lexington and Concord, residents of Islip Precinct met and signed the Articles of Association, a pledge to support the Continental Congress. Signing was dangerous, because it was treason against the Crown. Isaac Thompson of Sagtikos Manor signed even though he was a Crown magistrate and the precinct's supervisor.

Islip also organized to defend itself. A June 1775 census counted 375 residents and 64 men eligible for militia service. Rather than stay split between the Smithtown and Huntington companies, the precinct formed its own. In February 1776, Isaac Thompson wrote to the Provincial Congress asking for commissions for the officers the people had chosen, naming Benajah Strong as captain of a company of about 36 or 37 men.

The town's own minute book records the change from the inside. At the annual meeting of April 1775, Benajah Strong and Isaac Thompson were elected overseers of the highways, ordinary town jobs. A year later, in April 1776, the same meeting elected Thompson supervisor, and the clerk recorded that “A Gun and the accutriments” had been “paid for at Said Town Meeting which are the property of the towns.” The minutes never say what the gun was for. The town simply bought it, wrote it down, and went on with its business, three months before independence was declared.

That summer the war arrived in force. After the Declaration of Independence was read at Huntington in July 1776, Suffolk County militia marched west, likely under the Liberty flag raised at Huntington, toward the Battle of Long Island. The Continental Army was defeated in Brooklyn, and by September 1, 1776, British and Loyalist forces occupied Long Island. Islip would remain under Crown authority for six years.

The Revolution was not only something happening in Philadelphia or on distant battlefields. In Islip it entered houses, farms, roads, and family relationships. The question was not only who supported independence, but how people survived when the war came into their daily lives.

washingtons-visit.inBrief: In 1790, President George Washington stopped for one night at Sagtikos Manor during a tour of Long Island. He came not as a general, but as the leader of a brand-new country.
washingtons-visit.title: Washington's Visit
washingtons-visit.summary: A president's 1790 stop, and what local tradition tells.
sources: George J. Munkenbeck, “Isaac Thompson — A Man on a Tightrope.”
Michele Antonio, “George Washington Slept at Sagtikos Manor,” Patch, June 26, 2010 (updated June 27, 2010). Secondary local-history source.
body: In 1790, George Washington toured Long Island as the first President of the United States. The war was over and the new republic had begun, but the landscape he traveled still carried the memory of occupation, loyalty, danger, and survival. Local tradition places Washington at Sagtikos Manor for one night during that tour, and the same house is remembered in local tradition for the wartime presence, from time to time, of British General Henry Clinton during the occupation, though his exact stays need further research.

The deeper meaning of the visit has to be stated with care. Isaac Thompson was related by family to people connected with Revolutionary intelligence networks, and some modern accounts suggest Washington's tour was partly to thank those who had helped. That cannot be proven. This exhibit does not claim, as settled fact, that Washington stopped “to thank spies,” or that Isaac Thompson worked with the Culper Spy Ring. Those remain open research questions.

A careful telling is enough. Fourteen years after independence was declared, Washington returned to Long Island not as a general but as president, and his stop connected Islip's Revolutionary past to the new nation that followed.

an-unfinished-promise.inBrief: The Revolution spoke of liberty, but not everyone was free. Women, Black people, and Native people were left out, and enslaved people worked at Sagtikos Manor. The promise of independence was real, but it was not yet shared by all.
an-unfinished-promise.title: An Unfinished Promise
an-unfinished-promise.summary: Liberty's language, and the people it did not yet reach.
sources: New York State 250th Commemoration Field Guide.
George J. Munkenbeck, “The Origins of the Town of Islip.”
Nathaniel Conklin, “Description of the Town of Islip in Suffolk County,” January 11, 1798; transcribed and annotated by the Town of Islip Historian's Office.
George J. Munkenbeck, “Isaac Thompson — A Man on a Tightrope,” drawing on Christopher Verga's research on slavery in Suffolk County.
New York State, Laws of the State of New York, vol. 1 (Albany, 1886): the 1778 and 1779 acts.
New York State Senate and Assembly resolution on plundering, June 28, 1781.
Carl A. Starace, ed., Book One of the Minutes of Town Meetings and Register of Animal Ear Marks of the Town of Islip, 1720–1861 (Town of Islip, 1982); digitized by the Office of the Town Historian.
body: The Revolution spoke in the language of liberty, but that liberty did not reach everyone. When Islip signed the Articles of Association, only a portion of residents could take part. Enslaved and free Black people, Native people, women, children, and Quakers were excluded or unable to sign, and public power in early Islip belonged to a narrow group of men with property.

Occupation made loyalty itself dangerous, and the law followed. New York passed an act against people of “equivocal and suspected” character in 1778, and in 1779 it seized and sold the estates of those who sided with the Crown. Long Island residents were caught in the middle. A 1781 resolution complained that armed boats from Connecticut were plundering Islip's own shore in what became known as the Whaleboat War, treating occupied neighbors as enemy subjects.

Islip's own minute book shows how tangled those years were. The town met every April through the occupation, electing officers and caring for its poor, while dating its records, as legal documents in occupied territory had to be dated, by the reign of King George III. That wording does not mean Islip's people were Loyalists: the same meetings kept re-electing Isaac Thompson, a signer of the patriot Articles of Association, as supervisor. In 1783 the town voted support for Johanna Hutton and her child, boarded family to family in proportion to what each was rated. Only in April 1784 did the heading change, to “the Authority of the good People of the State of new york” and “the Eighth year of the American Independence.”

Sagtikos Manor belongs in this harder part of the story too. Much of the work on the farm and in the home was done by enslaved and indentured people. Records indicate that Isaac Thompson held, on average, about four enslaved people in the decades around 1790, 1800, and 1810, and the fuller story of those individuals is part of the ongoing 250th research.
This does not weaken the Revolutionary story; it makes it more honest. The promise was real, but it was not equally shared. In Islip, as in the nation, independence was a beginning, not an ending.

many-roots-one-town.inBrief: After the Revolution, Islip kept growing as new families and communities made it their home. The Jewish community of Islip is one strong example of how the town's story kept widening to include more people.
many-roots-one-town.title: Many Roots, One Town
many-roots-one-town.summary: The communities that kept widening who belongs.
sources: Mollie Sebor, “The Story of the Jewish Community of Islip Town” (Girl Scout Gold Award booklet).
Nathaniel Conklin, “Description of the Town of Islip in Suffolk County,” January 11, 1798; transcribed and annotated by Christopher Albergo and George J. Munkenbeck, Office of the Town Historian.
Carl A. Starace, ed., Book One of the Minutes of Town Meetings and Register of Animal Ear Marks of the Town of Islip, 1720–1861 (Town of Islip, 1982); digitized by the Office of the Town Historian.
body: The promise of independence kept unfolding long after the Revolution, because the meaning of “the people” kept expanding. Over generations, new families and communities entered Islip's civic, religious, and cultural life and built institutions that made belonging visible.

The roots reach back to the town's very first records. In January 1798, Supervisor Nathaniel Conklin counted about 120 dwelling houses in Islip and wrote that twenty-five of them, about one in every five, were homes where “Indians, Mustees and free Negros” lived; “Mustee” was a period word for people of mixed ancestry, commonly Native American and African. The town minutes tell the same story through individual lives: in 1740 Joseph Nicolls, whom the clerk recorded as “Mallator,” meaning mulatto, agreed with the town to board a poor neighbor for two shillings a week, and in 1787 the town bought grain for a woman the clerk called “Indian hannah.” Early Islip was never a town of one people.

One clear example is the Jewish community of Islip Town. A local history records that Jews have lived in Islip since at least the end of the Civil War, tracing the community through the Bay Shore United Hebrew Benevolent Cemetery Association recognized in 1897, the first synagogue in 1918, and the later growth of congregations in Bay Shore, Brentwood, Central Islip, Oakdale, East Islip, and Sayville. It also records Holocaust survivors who found refuge in Islip after World War II, among them Samuel Sitko.

This kind of community history is the bridge between the Revolution and the present. The Revolution created a language of rights and belonging; later generations had to make that promise real in local life. In time, this panel should hold other communities as well, including Italian, Hispanic and Latino, Polish, Central American, Dutch, Irish, African American, and Indigenous families, as the sources become ready.

the-promise-continues.inBrief: History is not only the past. Today, people choose what to save, question, and share. This project is part of that work: keeping Islip's story open to everyone.
the-promise-continues.title: The Promise Continues
the-promise-continues.summary: Why this history is still being written.
sources: New York State 250th Commemoration Field Guide.
Town of Islip Historian's Office, Vignettes, 2024 special issue.
Town of Islip Historian's Office, Vignettes, 2025 special issue.
Carl A. Starace, ed., Book One of the Minutes of Town Meetings and Register of Animal Ear Marks of the Town of Islip, 1720–1861 (Town of Islip, 1982); digitized by the Office of the Town Historian.
Nathaniel Conklin, “Description of the Town of Islip in Suffolk County,” January 11, 1798; transcribed and annotated by Christopher Albergo and George J. Munkenbeck, Office of the Town Historian.
body: History is not only what happened long ago. It is also what a community chooses to preserve, question, and share. The New York State Field Guide describes the anniversary as a chance for the field of history to help communities engage with the Revolution in all its complexity and continuing meaning.

That work is already happening in Islip. The Town Historian's Office runs an internship and apprenticeship program that teaches research, writing, preservation, and public presentation, and this digital exhibit shares that purpose. With the Sons of the American Revolution, the Office has researched and marked the burial places of Islip's known patriots, and a historic “Islip Militia” marker is planned near Town Hall, among the first steps in the Town's 250th commemoration.

Digitizing the town's records is part of keeping the promise. The gun bought at the April 1776 town meeting, the wartime votes to care for Johanna Hutton and her child, the 1784 heading that first counted the years by American independence, the twenty-five households Nathaniel Conklin counted in 1798: each survived as a few handwritten lines in the town's old records. Scanning, transcribing, and publishing those pages is what lets a student or a neighbor find them today, and what makes it possible to keep asking who Islip's story has included.

The promise of independence continues when more people can enter the story. That is why this exhibit is digital, bilingual, and written plainly. A student, a parent, a new resident, a lifelong neighbor, or a researcher should all be able to find a place in Islip's past. The story is not finished. Every document preserved, every family story shared, and every correction offered becomes part of the town's public memory.
